experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I have no concrete role information yet; as a Servant, all players except me are unknown. It is round 1 on quest 1 with a 2-person team proposed by me: [0,1]. Early in the game, approving a team containing myself is standard because it gathers information with low downside, and rejecting without evidence slows good more than it helps. My approve vote is expected and reveals little beyond confidence in testing an opening team.
Player 1: As a Servant, I have no private information yet. On round 1 with quest 1 requiring 2 players, approving an initial small team that includes myself is standard because there is no evidence against 0, and early approvals avoid unnecessary rejection cycles. My vote reveals little beyond a willingness to test the first team, which is generally good for the Good side.
Player 2: I am evil Assassin with teammate 4. Proposed team [0,1] contains no evil players, so if approved the quest likely passes and gives good side an early success. On round 1, rejecting a clean team is generally good for evil because it can steer toward including 2 or 4 later without revealing much; early rejections are not too informative. Optimal evil play is to reject.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil, so the proposed team [0,1] contains no known evil and is strong for Quest 1. As good, I want early successful teams while avoiding signaling too strongly; approving a clean 2-person team is standard and does not reveal special information.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are not confirmed beyond likely good. In round 1 on a 2-player team that excludes all evil, approving risks a clean success that builds trust between 0 and 1 and gives good early momentum. As evil, I generally want to reject teams with no evil members, especially early when rejections carry little cost. My vote reveals limited information since early round-1 rejects are common and not strongly alignment-indicative.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], so 0 and 1 are somewhat more trusted, though one could still be evil. Player 2 is less known. As a Good Servant in team voting, I should generally approve reasonable teams that include the successful prior team plus one new player to gather information. Approving [0,1,2] is standard and does not give evil special leverage beyond normal info gain.
Player 1: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], which gives mild positive evidence on 0 and stronger evidence on myself as Good, but does not clear 0 because evil can pass early. There is little hard info on player 2 yet. As a Good Servant in team voting, I want to approve reasonable teams that include at least one previously successful member and avoid excessive rejections early. This team [0,1,2] keeps both members from the successful first quest and only adds one unknown, which is a solid, information-gathering team for a 3-person quest. Approving does not reveal much beyond that I support a sensible continuation, and rejecting would be too conservative with current evidence.
Player 2: I know Player 4 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1,2] contains only one evil player: me. Quest 1 succeeded with [0,1], which gives little hard info except that at least one of 0/1 is likely good or an evil chose to pass. In team voting as evil, the optimal play is usually to approve teams that include an evil player so we have influence on the quest and can shape perceptions. Approving also looks natural since the leader added themselves plus one prior successful member and me; rejecting could draw suspicion. My vote itself reveals limited information, and approving helps evil more than rejecting here because I am on the team and can decide the quest outcome later if it passes.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know players 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,2] includes confirmed evil player 2, so from a good-team perspective this team is highly likely to fail and should be rejected. As Merlin, rejecting a team with a hidden reason is normal and does not reveal too much by itself, especially since some players may also distrust adding a new player after a successful [0,1] team.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me, while 0 and 1 just succeeded together on quest 1, making them somewhat more likely good. The current phase is team voting; as evil, I generally want to reject teams containing my teammate if approving would strongly implicate them later, but on quest 2 a 3-person team with one evil can fail and swing the game to 1-1. Approving a team with player 2 gives evil a chance to sabotage now, and my approval is not especially revealing because mixed votes are common.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Quest 1 with [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and/or 1 looked fine then. Quest 2 with [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, so at least one of {0,1,2} is evil; since I know I am good, that means at least one of {1,2} is evil. Team [2,4] includes suspicious 2 plus unknown 4, and excludes me and 1, giving weak confidence for a pivotal quest 3. As a good servant in team voting, optimal play is to reject teams with a likely evil member when better information-supported alternatives exist. Rejecting also signals suspicion of 2 without locking in bad quest composition.
Player 1: I know Quest 1 with team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 look somewhat better. Quest 2 with team [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, so one of {0,1,2} is evil; since I know I am good, one of {0,2} is evil. Proposed team [2,4] excludes me and includes 2, who is a prime suspect; 4 is unknown. As Good, optimal play is to reject teams that are likely to contain evil, especially on Quest 3 after a fail has already occurred. Rejecting reveals I am not willing to trust a highly suspicious team, which helps good coordination more than approving an unsafe team.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, and I was on the failed Quest 2 team with 0 and 1, so at least one of us looked suspicious there while 4 stayed off-team. Now on Quest 3, the proposed team is exactly the two evil players [2,4], which gives evil guaranteed control of the quest if approved. In team voting as evil, I should strongly support any team that includes enough evil to fail, especially this one. Approving also minimally reveals information because evil generally wants such teams to pass, and my vote alone is not too informative.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are Evil. The current phase is team voting on proposed team [2,4], which is certainly bad because it consists entirely of Evil players and would almost guarantee a failed quest. As Good, I should reject. Voting reject is also information-consistent without exposing Merlin too strongly, since any reasonable Good player should oppose a highly suspicious team after player 2 was on the failed quest and now proposes themself plus 4.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, which is consistent with 2 failing and leaves 0/1 likely good. Current phase is team voting on [2,4], a 2-player team containing both evil players. As evil, the optimal play is to approve a team that can guarantee a fail and potentially look somewhat self-serving but still powerful. My approve gives this team the best chance to pass. Voting approve reveals mild suspicion on me if tracked, but securing a likely failed quest is worth it.

Rejecting aligns with prior evidence too: 4 is evil and 2/4 pairing was already suspicious.
Player 4: I know Player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, strongly implicating 2 or allowing suspicion on 0/1; since 2 is evil, that team was good for us. Current proposal [0,4] includes me and excludes 2, so if approved I can fail the quest and put strong suspicion on myself or 0. As evil on the team, the optimal play is to approve a team containing me so I have agency over the quest result. My approve vote may mildly associate me with the team, but securing an evil-controlled quest is worth it.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Two failed 2-player quests each had exactly one evil: [2,4] and [0,4]. Their overlap makes 4 highly suspicious; since I'm Good, player 2 is also likely evil from the first fail. That suggests 0 and 3 are plausible good, but this proposed 3-player team [0,2,3] includes suspicious player 2. At 1-2, approving a team likely containing evil risks an immediate loss. As a Servant, I should reject unsafe teams; my vote signals distrust of 2 and avoids helping evil.
Player 1: As a Good Servant, I know only from behavior. Two failed 2-person quests identify both members on each failed team as containing at least one evil: [0,4] had a fail and [0,2,3] now combines 0 with 2 and 3, where 2 was on the earlier failed [2,4]. Across the two failed 2-person quests, likely evils are among 0,2,4, with player 0 especially suspicious for appearing on one failed quest and now proposing a team that excludes the known-good me after prior rejection patterns. On quest 4, approving a 3-person team with highly suspicious players 0 and 2 is very risky for Good because Evil needs one more fail to win. Rejecting preserves chance for a safer team. My vote signals distrust of a team built from failed-quest players, which helps Good more than approving a dangerous lineup.
Player 2: I am evil Assassin with teammate 4. Score is Evil 2 - Good 1, so approving a team containing me is strong because I can fail this quest and likely win immediately. Team [0,2,3] excludes my teammate but includes me; as long as I’m on it, evil controls at least one fail. Approving also looks somewhat plausible since player 0 included me after prior fails, and rejecting a team with myself could be suspicious. Best for evil is to approve.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] includes confirmed evil player 2, so it should be rejected. At 1-2 heading into quest 4, approving a team with known evil is disastrous. My reject is consistent with good play and does not reveal more than necessary, since any reasonable good player should oppose a team containing a suspicious/failed-quest player like 2.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me. This proposed team [0,2,3] includes one known evil (2) and excludes me. Since evil already leads 2-1, approving a team with 2 gives a good chance to secure the winning third fail. Voting approve is optimal for evil and my vote itself gives limited additional information because approvals on mixed teams are common.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: We know quest 3 team [0,4] had exactly one fail, so either 4 is evil or I am, and I know I am good, so 4 is confirmed evil. Current proposed team [1,2,3] excludes confirmed evil 4, but we still need to identify the second evil among 1,2,3. Since quests 1 and 2 also failed, and 2 proposed [2,4] while 1 now proposes [1,2,3], there is substantial suspicion on 2 and possibly 1 or 3. Approving a 3-person team made entirely of uncertain players when good is down 1-2 is too risky; as good, I should reject until a team includes me and excludes confirmed evil/suspicious combinations. My reject signals that [1,2,3] is not sufficiently trustworthy.
Player 1: I know only that team [0,4] produced a fail, so at least one of 0/4 is evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both suspected players, and as a Servant on a must-win quest for Good, approving a team that avoids the only directly implicated pair is optimal. My vote signals support for testing the untainted trio, which helps Good gather clean information and possibly survive.
Player 2: I know player 4 is evil with me. Quest 3 team [0,4] failed with one fail, strongly implicating 4 or 0; since I know 4 is evil, that failure is explained. Current proposed team [1,2,3] includes me but not 4. As evil on quest 4 with evil already at 2 quest wins, approving a team with me on it gives us a direct chance to win by failing the quest. Rejecting risks a later team without any evil. My vote reveals some willingness to go on a team, but at this stage securing the quest is more important than subtlety.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know player 2 is Evil. Proposed team [1,2,3] includes confirmed Evil, so approving is very likely to hand Evil another fail and lose the game. In team voting, I should reject any team containing known Evil when possible. Rejecting also subtly signals suspicion without outing myself too directly.
Player 4: I know Player 2 is evil with me. The proposed team [1,2,3] includes my evil teammate but not me. Since evil only needs one more failed quest to win and this is quest 4 with team size 3, approving a team with Player 2 gives us a strong chance to fail it. Voting approve is optimal for evil. My vote reveals limited info because approving a team with mixed suspicion is plausible, and securing an evil player on the team helps my side most.
